title: Enabling quantum communication in ultra-large-scale networks
abstract: The recent development of small-scale quantum networks poses the question of whether such a technology could also operate at scale in the futuristic Quantum Internet. The question can be answered with a classical approach where an arbitrary quantum network is represented as a classical graph, and communication reliability is assessed using methods proper of network theory. Unfortunately, sufficient conditions for viable network-wide communication have been established only for special topologies like regular lattices. No practical communication protocols have been developed so far for real network topologies, if not for relatively small networks. Here, we overcome these limitations by devising a family of quantum communication protocols that can be applied to networks with arbitrary topology, composed of even hundreds of millions nodes. By performing a systematic analysis on both real and synthetic graphs, we show that the proposed protocols are sustainable on heterogeneous networks. For random scale-free graphs, we analytically prove that viable quantum communication persists in the thermodynamic limit. Our findings provide evidence that the Quantum Internet will be capable of sustaining a ultra-large-scale growth comparable to one already experienced by its classical predecessor.
summary: This paper develops novel quantum communication protocols designed to function across arbitrary, ultra-large-scale network topologies. By employing techniques like entanglement swapping and distillation, the authors prove that these protocols remain viable even in complex scale-free networks as they approach infinite size. The findings suggest a path toward realizing a functional Quantum Internet.
main_problem: The central problem is determining if quantum communication can be reliably sustained at ultra-large scales, comparable to the growth of a global Internet.
main_result: The proposed specialized protocols, particularly $h_x	ext{QEP}$, are shown to maintain viable quantum communication in the thermodynamic limit for scale-free random graphs.
method: The authors devise and analyze a family of quantum communication protocols involving entanglement swapping and distillation, applying them systematically to arbitrary graph topologies.
model_or_system: The system is modeled as an abstract quantum network represented by a classical weighted graph where edge weights quantify the available entanglement. The analysis focuses on extending results beyond special topologies like regular lattices.
key_observables: Achievable average entanglement ($\langle E_{	ext{prot}} angle$) and the Area Under the Curve (AUC) are used as primary performance metrics to compare protocols.
important_parameters: The analysis considers varying edge entanglement ($E_{	ext{edge}}$), network size ($N$), redundancy ($k$), and geodesic distance ($\ell$).
main_assumptions: Previous work was limited to special topologies; the current approach assumes quantum operations can be mapped onto transformations on classical graph edges.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates protocol performance (QEP, $h_x	ext{QEP}$) on a small network (Karate Club). Figure 2 shows the decay of AUC for standard protocols on random graphs as N increases. Figure 3 compares protocol performance across various real networks based on degree heterogeneity.
paper_structure: The paper systematically develops quantum communication protocols (QEP, CEP, $h_x	ext{QEP}$) by defining their underlying operations (swapping/distillation). It then analyzes these protocols theoretically and numerically on increasingly complex graph models to prove scalability in the thermodynamic limit.
why_interesting: This work provides rigorous theoretical evidence for scaling quantum networking beyond idealized structures, which is crucial for designing future large-scale quantum infrastructure.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Quantum communication on a real network. (a) As an illustrative example, we consider the Zachary Karate Club’s network [52]. (b) We set the entanglement of the individual edges to Eedge = 0.60, then we apply the QEP protocol to establish a communication channel between the nodes s = 17 and t = 27, both highlighted in red. Edges utilized by the protocol are visualized with a color different from gray. In particular, all edges belonging to the same path selected by the QEP protocol have the same color. The resulting entanglement is EQEP s,t = 0.41. In this case, the CEP protocol selects the very same paths as QEP, however, the resulting entanglement is ECEP s,t = 0.12. (c) Same as...

Figure 2. Quantum communication on synthetic networks. (a) We measure the Area Under the Curve [AUC, see Eq. (13)] for the vari- ous communication protocols on random graphs with power-law de- gree distribution K(k) ∼k−γ. The AUC metric is plotted as a function of the network size N for γ = 2.1. Results are averaged over mul- tiple instances of the network model, and many randomly selected pairs of nodes s and t, see SM for details. (b-d) Same as in (a), but for γ = 2.5, 3.5 and 4.5, respectively.

Figure 3. Quantum communication on real networks. We display with small red circles the relative improvement AUCQEP/AUCCEP −1 of QEP vs. CEP for 101 real networks. Each network is further char- acterized by a different level of degree heterogeneity ⟨k2⟩/⟨k⟩2 used as abscissa in the plot. To highlight the trend, we group data using logarithmic binning and display with large red circles median values for each of the bins. Black and purple triangles display analogous re- sults, but for the comparisons h1QEP vs. QEP and h2QEP vs. QEP, respectively.
